what: what is a single page application
why: why do react apps not reload the page
compare: single page app vs multi page app

1_beginner_explanation.summary: Historically, clicking a link to an 'About' page would destroy the current webpage, send a request to the server, and download a completely new `about.html` file (Multi-Page App). A **Single Page Application (SPA)** downloads exactly ONE HTML file ever (`index.html`). When you click a link, JavaScript steps in, says 'Stop! Don't refresh the page!', magically erases the old content, and draws the new 'About' content instantly using JS.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: An MPA is like reading a book; you turn the physical page to see the next chapter. An SPA is like reading an Etch A Sketch; instead of turning the page, you wipe it clean and draw the next chapter on the exact same piece of glass.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: 1. User clicks `<a href="/about">`. 2. JS intercepts the click event and calls `e.preventDefault()`. 3. JS uses the History API (`history.pushState()`) to fake the URL change in the browser bar so the user thinks they went to a new page. 4. JS destroys DOM nodes and builds new ones.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Because the page never actually refreshes, Memory Leaks are a massive problem in SPAs. If you attach an Event Listener on the Home page, and navigate to the About page without deleting that listener, it stays in memory forever.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Creating a seamless, app-like experience where music keeps playing while you navigate (like Spotify).

4_common_confusion.trap: Thinking the URL in the browser bar always represents a real file on the server. In an SPA, if the URL says `website.com/dashboard/settings`, the file `settings.html` does not exist on the server! JS is just faking the URL.
4_common_confusion.fix: This means your Server MUST be configured to always return `index.html` no matter what URL the user requests, otherwise they will get a 404 error if they hit 'Refresh'.

6_edge_case.quirk: Because JS fakes the URL change, the browser's native 'Back' and 'Forward' buttons break by default. SPAs must carefully listen to the `popstate` window event to manually redraw the page when the user hits the Back button.
